Mental Status: alert, oriented x 3. attentive. Speech normal. Intact repetition . Able to follow both midline and appendicular commands. Pt. was able to register 3 objects but recalls [**1-17**] at 5 minutes. The pt. had good knowledge of current events.  There was no evidence of apraxia or neglect
PERRL 3 to 2mm and brisk.  VFF . Fundus: Mild blurring of disc on L only

MRV of head: Venous sinus thrombosis involving the left transverse sinus, sigmoid sinus, perisplenial veins, and straight sinus as described in detail  above. Areas of restricted diffusion, consistent with ischemia, possibly  subacute involving the thalamus, basal ganglia, splenium of the corpus  callosum, caudate nucleus, and bifrontal subcortical white matter
MRV OF THE HEAD: There is lack of flow throughout the straight sinus, left transverse sinus, and sigmoid sinus, consistent with venous sinus thrombosis

MRI of head: Venous sinus thrombosis involving the left transverse sinus, sigmoid sinus, perisplenial veins, and straight sinus as described in detail above. Areas of restricted diffusion, consistent with ischemia, possibly subacute involving the thalamus, basal ganglia, splenium of the corpus callosum, caudate nucleus, and bifrontal subcortical white matter
RHW (G2P2)
retro-orbital headache
analgesics
MRI/V as outpatient which showed venous sinus thrombosis
underewent further imaging which showed venous sinus thrombosis of straight sinus, left transverse sinus, and sigmoid sinus
Both MRI showed areas of restricted diffusion involving the thalamus, basal ganglia, splenium of the corpus  callosum, caudate nucleus, and bifrontal subcortical white matter

These areas of restricted diffusion were likely due to ischemia or edema due to lack of venous drainage as a result of the venous sinus thrombosis - however, it seemed unlikely that these were areas of completed stroke (due to the lack of neurological deficits on exam).
Patient was initially admitted to the ICU then transferred to the floor
Other than headache, the patient had normal exam with no neurological deficits
Heparin gtt and Coumadin

lupus anti-coagulant, anti cardiolipin antibodies, Protein C and S, beta 2-microglobulin
anti-thrombin III deficiency
heparin gtt bridging with Coumadin
prevent expansion of the clot
switched to Lovenox
Patient's headache was much improved by discharge
